section.name: core-configuration
section.summary: Essential tmux.conf settings: prefix key, mouse support, vi copy-mode, pane/window navigation bindings, and status bar setup.

rule.statement: Use Ctrl+A as the prefix key rather than the default Ctrl+B; it is more ergonomic and consistent with GNU screen muscle memory.
rule.rationale: Ctrl+B requires moving the left hand off the home row. Ctrl+A is reachable without moving the hand, reducing friction across hundreds of daily prefix-chord invocations.

rule.statement: Set base-index 1 and pane-base-index 1; the default zero-indexing makes Alt+1 select window 0, which feels counter-intuitive.
rule.rationale: With base-index 1, Alt+1 through Alt+9 map directly to the visual position of windows in the status bar.

rule.statement: Always set escape-time to 10ms or less; the default 500ms causes a noticeable delay in vim when pressing Escape.
rule.rationale: Vim and other editors rely on fast Escape detection. The default 500ms makes modal editing feel sluggish over SSH.

example.code:
# Prefix: Ctrl+A (ergonomic, screen-compatible muscle memory) set -g prefix C-a unbind C-b bind C-a send-prefix
# Enable full mouse (scroll, click panes, resize, select text) set -g mouse on
# Vi mode for copy (Escape-free text selection in scroll buffer) setw -g mode-keys vi bind -T copy-mode-vi v send-keys -X begin-selection bind -T copy-mode-vi y send-keys -X copy-selection-and-cancel
# 1-based indexing for intuitive Alt+number window switching set -g base-index 1 setw -g pane-base-index 1 set -g renumber-windows on
# Faster escape for vim set -sg escape-time 10 set -g focus-events on
# Large scrollback set -g history-limit 50000

rule.statement: Use | and - for splits; the defaults " and % are not mnemonic. Open splits in the current pane's working directory.
rule.rationale: | visually represents a vertical divider (horizontal split). - represents a horizontal line (vertical split). Both are immediately memorable. Current-path inheritance eliminates the need to cd after each split.

example.code:
# Intuitive splits that inherit the current directory bind | split-window -h -c "#{pane_current_path}" bind - split-window -v -c "#{pane_current_path}" unbind '"' unbind %
# New window in current directory bind c new-window -c "#{pane_current_path}"
# Pane navigation without prefix (Ctrl+arrows) bind -n C-Up select-pane -U bind -n C-Down select-pane -D bind -n C-Left select-pane -L bind -n C-Right select-pane -R
# Pane resize with prefix + H/J/K/L (vi directions) bind -r H resize-pane -L 5 bind -r J resize-pane -D 5 bind -r K resize-pane -U 5 bind -r L resize-pane -R 5
# Window switching with Alt+number (no prefix needed) bind -n M-1 select-window -t 1 bind -n M-2 select-window -t 2 bind -n M-3 select-window -t 3 bind -n M-4 select-window -t 4 bind -n M-5 select-window -t 5
# Reload config without restarting tmux bind r source-file ~/.tmux.conf \; display "Config reloaded"

section.name: status-bar

rule.statement: Run the status bar script with a 5-second refresh interval; 1-second intervals cause noticeable CPU overhead on low-power VPS instances.
rule.rationale: A status bar that refreshes every second calls shell scripts 60 times per minute. At 5 seconds, the overhead drops to 12 calls per minute with no perceptible staleness for CPU/memory metrics.

example.code:
set -g status-position bottom set -g status-style "bg=colour235,fg=colour248" set -g status-interval 5
# Left: session name with color accent set -g status-left-length 30 set -g status-left "#[fg=colour0,bg=colour4,bold] #S #[fg=colour4,bg=colour235,nobold] "
# Right: system metrics script + time set -g status-right-length 120 set -g status-right '#(~/.tmux-system.sh) #[fg=colour240]| #[fg=colour248]%H:%M '
# Pane border colors (dim inactive, bright active) set -g pane-border-style "fg=colour240" set -g pane-active-border-style "fg=colour4"
# Terminal true-color support set -g default-terminal "screen-256color" set -ga terminal-overrides ",xterm-256color:RGB"

section.reference: Status bar script: CPU/memory/disk with color-coded thresholds
